metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Scope entry for `outbound_fields.py`: either (a) remove it and add `applier.py` as also importing from `outbound_fields.py`, or (b) clarify that the entry describes the coupling direction and that no code change to `outbound_fields.py` is required (only `applier.py` gains the import).
findings.finding: outbound_fields.py is listed in Scope as a file to be modified ('import `_map_local_to_jira_fields` (new cross-module coupling)'), but the plan body (Step 2) only describes CALLING the existing function from applier.py — no actual change to outbound_fields.py is described. The coupling arrow points the wrong direction: applier.py imports FROM outbound_fields.py, so applier.py is the file that changes, not outbound_fields.py. This contradicts the Scope list and may cause an implementer to make unnecessary or incorrect edits to outbound_fields.py.
findings.location: Scope file list: '`src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/outbound_fields.py` — import `_map_local_to_jira_fields` (new cross-module coupling…)'; Step 2: 'map it via `outbound_fields._map_local_to_jira_fields(ticket, binding_store, …)` (outbound_fields.py:112-117)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the outbound_fields.py Scope bullet with: '`src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/applier.py` gains a new import of `_map_local_to_jira_fields` from `outbound_fields.py` (new cross-module coupling: inbound consumer -> outbound field mapper; acceptable, it is the canonical mapper).' Remove `outbound_fields.py` from the Scope file list unless an actual change to that file is needed (e.g., making a private function public).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify Step 3 (Wiring): replace the `pending_bug_tickets` analogy with the accurate description — inject the re-create dict directly into `outbound_or_untyped` (before L484) or append a pre-converted dict to `outbound_list` (after L486), NOT follow a finally-block drain pattern; `pending_bug_tickets` bypasses `_apply_batch` entirely and is an incorrect prior-art reference for this injection.
findings.finding: The plan's Step 3 / Wiring section says 'Follow the exact pattern applier.py already uses for `pending_bug_tickets`', but `pending_bug_tickets` is drained in a `finally:` block AFTER `_apply_batch` (L503-521), whereas the plan's actual proposed wiring is to APPEND to `outbound_list` BEFORE `_apply_batch` (L484-501). These are structurally different patterns with different semantics: the `pending_bug_tickets` pattern bypasses `_apply_batch` entirely and calls a side-channel directly, while the plan's proposed CREATE injection must flow into `_apply_batch` to get `create_one`'s JQL dedup + `bind_confirm` behaviour. An implementer following 'follow the exact pattern' literally would wire a post-batch finally-block rather than a pre-batch list injection, breaking the intended create_one flow.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the wiring description to: 'On `follow_on.get("action")=="create_after_hard_delete"`, build the outbound CREATE as a plain dict (action="create", key=local_id, fields=…, local_id=local_id) and `outbound_or_untyped.append(it)` before the `outbound_list` comprehension at L484, so it is automatically included in the list passed to `_apply_batch`. The `pending_bug_tickets` pattern is not analogous — it drains in a `finally:` block and never enters `_apply_batch`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In Step 3, replace the `pending_bug_tickets` prior-art rationale with the correct structural model: introduce a dedicated `pending_hard_delete_creates: list[dict]` side-channel (analogous to `pending_bug_tickets` in declaration/population pattern), populate it during the inbound loop when `follow_on.get('action') == 'create_after_hard_delete'`, then `outbound_list.extend(pending_hard_delete_creates)` BEFORE the `_apply_batch` call at L495 — explicitly noting this differs from `pending_bug_tickets` which drains post-batch.
findings.finding: The plan cites `pending_bug_tickets` (declared L453, appended L470-474, 'drained after dispatch') as the prior art / structural model for how to inject the synthetic CREATE Mutation into the same pass, but `pending_bug_tickets` is actually drained in the `finally` block AFTER `_apply_batch` returns (applier.py:503-521) via a separate `in_process_cli` call — it is the exact opposite pattern (post-batch side-effect, not pre-batch list injection). The plan's textual instruction (append to `outbound_list` BEFORE `_apply_batch`) is correct, but an implementer who 'follows the exact pattern' of `pending_bug_tickets` will drain the hard-delete creates post-batch, bypassing `create_one`'s JQL dedup and `bind_confirm` in the same pass and breaking the plan's own correctness invariants.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace Step 3's prior-art paragraph with: 'Introduce `pending_hard_delete_creates: list[dict] = []` before the inbound loop (parallel to `pending_bug_tickets`). During the loop, when `follow_on.get("action") == "create_after_hard_delete"`, build the fields and append the CREATE dict to `pending_hard_delete_creates`. After the loop, call `outbound_list.extend(pending_hard_delete_creates)` BEFORE the `_apply_batch` call at L495. Note: unlike `pending_bug_tickets` (which is drained post-batch in a `finally` block via `in_process_cli`), this side-channel must feed INTO `outbound_list` pre-batch so `create_one`\'s JQL dedup and `bind_confirm` run within the normal sequencer.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] For each 'tested' or 'verified' AC (#2, #3, #4, #6), replace the hedge with a concrete proving command or a named test function (e.g., `pytest tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_applier_hard_delete.py::test_no_create_when_ticket_absent -v`) so done-ness is mechanically verifiable.
findings.finding: Five of the eight ACs use only the word 'tested' or 'verified' as their proving command (ACs #3, #4, #6, and partially #2), with no concrete command (e.g., `pytest tests/unit/…::test_name -v`) or structured assertion anchor. Per E6, every completion-relevant claim needs a concrete proving command that produces evidence on success, not a hedge.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC #2 ('verified it flows through create_one'), AC #3 ('tested'), AC #4 ('Both partial-failure directions tested'), AC #6 ('tested')
findings.suggested_fix: Template: 'AC #3: `pytest tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_applier_hard_delete.py::test_skip_when_local_absent -v` exits 0 and its captured log output contains "skipping re-create".'  Apply the same pattern to ACs #2, #4, #6.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Scope/files entry for `outbound_fields.py`: the file needs no edits — move the cross-module coupling note to the `applier.py` entry, which is where the new `from rebar_reconciler.outbound_fields import _map_local_to_jira_fields` import actually lives.
findings.finding: outbound_fields.py is listed in the Scope/files section as an edit target, but no changes to outbound_fields.py are described anywhere in the plan — the new import of _map_local_to_jira_fields lives in applier.py, not outbound_fields.py.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove outbound_fields.py from the Scope/files list and add a note to the applier.py entry: '+ new import: `from rebar_reconciler.outbound_fields import _map_local_to_jira_fields` (new cross-module coupling; outbound_fields.py itself needs no changes)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify Step 3: specify the exact injection point and object type — either inject the re-create as a typed `Mutation` into `outbound_or_untyped` BEFORE the list comprehension at L484 (so `_mutation_to_batch_dict` auto-converts it), or build a raw dict with keys `{"action":"create", "key":local_id, "fields":{…}, "local_id":local_id}` and append it to `outbound_list` after L486.
findings.finding: The plan says (Step 3): 'build a STANDARD outbound CREATE Mutation (direction outbound, action create, payload={"local_id":…, "fields":…}) and APPEND it to outbound_list (applier.py:484-490)'. However, `outbound_list` at lines 484-486 is already a fully materialized `list[dict]` produced by a list comprehension that calls `_mutation_to_batch_dict` on any typed `Mutation` objects in `outbound_or_untyped`. A typed `Mutation` appended to `outbound_list` AFTER line 486 is a raw object, not a dict — `_apply_batch` iterates the list and calls `dispatch_mutation(mutation, ctx)` which expects dicts (it calls `mutation.get('action', '')` etc.). The plan does not specify whether to append a typed `Mutation` (then missing auto-conversion) or a raw dict (then skipping `_mutation_to_batch_dict`), nor whether to inject before or after the list comprehension.
findings.suggested_fix: Specify: 'Append a typed Mutation(direction=outbound, action=create, target=local_id, payload={"fields":…, "local_id":…}) to `outbound_or_untyped` (before the list comprehension at L484), so the existing `_looks_like_mutation` / `_mutation_to_batch_dict` path converts it automatically.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify Step 3: specify that the re-create entry must be appended to `outbound_list` as a **dict** (i.e. via `_mutation_to_batch_dict(mutation)` or by constructing the dict directly in the `{action, fields, local_id, direction, …}` batch-dict shape), not as a raw `Mutation` object.
findings.finding: Step 3 says to 'build a STANDARD outbound CREATE Mutation (direction outbound, action create, payload={…}) and APPEND it to outbound_list', but by the time outbound_list is populated (applier.py:484-490) it is already a list[dict] — appending a typed Mutation object without calling _mutation_to_batch_dict first will deliver a non-dict to _apply_batch's dispatch_mutation, which expects dict-shaped entries.
findings.location: Step 3 (Wiring) — 'build a STANDARD outbound CREATE Mutation … APPEND it to outbound_list (applier.py:484-490)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'build a STANDARD outbound CREATE Mutation … and APPEND it to outbound_list' with: 'build a batch-dict `{"action": "create", "direction": "outbound", "local_id": local_id, "fields": fields}` and `outbound_list.append(batch_dict)` — matching the shape `_apply_batch`/`dispatch_mutation` expects.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC1 to REQUIRE a test asserting that a `probe_outcome`-keyed payload (without `reason`) still reaches the hard_delete branch — remove the 'or switch to `reason`' escape hatch, or make both keys mandatory in the parametrized fixture.
findings.finding: The plan explicitly implements `probe_outcome` back-compat in `_apply_inbound_delete` (`payload.get("reason") or payload.get("probe_outcome", ...)`) but AC1 offers only an OR — 'parametrize both keys, or switch to `reason`' — meaning the fallback branch may be left entirely untested. If implementers choose 'switch to `reason`' without a back-compat test, the old key silently stops working and no test catches it.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC1, replace 'parametrize both keys, or switch to `reason`' with 'parametrize both keys' and add an explicit sub-criterion: 'a unit test asserts the hard_delete branch is also entered from a legacy `probe_outcome`-keyed payload (back-compat path is exercised)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Wrap the `.cache.json` read in a `try/except (json.JSONDecodeError, OSError)` block in the applier consumer and treat a parse failure as equivalent to 'ticket absent' (log + skip), so a corrupt cache file does not abort the entire reconcile pass.
findings.finding: The plan directs reading `.tickets-tracker/<local_id>/.cache.json` directly and handles the absent-file case (log + skip), but does not specify error handling for a malformed/corrupt JSON file. An unhandled `json.JSONDecodeError` (or similar parse exception) raised inside the applier's inbound loop would propagate upward and abort the entire reconcile pass rather than skipping just the one hard-delete item.
findings.location: Step 2 (Scope: applier.py inbound loop, applier.py:455-474) / AC line: 'Consumer failure path: local ticket absent / no mappable summary -> log + skip'
findings.suggested_fix: In the applier's `create_after_hard_delete` handler, wrap the file read+parse in a try/except block: `try: ticket = json.loads(path.read_text()); except (OSError, json.JSONDecodeError) as e: log.warning('cache unreadable for %s: %s', local_id, e); continue` — treating parse errors identically to absent files.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the prior-art citation in Step 3: acknowledge that `pending_bug_tickets` share only the side-channel *collection* pattern, not the drain site; explicitly justify the pre-batch `outbound_list` append as a novel drain site and analyze how HeadDriftError / RescheduleError mid-batch affects the injected CREATE.
findings.finding: The plan cites `pending_bug_tickets` as the 'exact pattern' of prior art for the side-channel + drain approach, but the code shows `pending_bug_tickets` are drained AFTER `_apply_batch` via `in_process_cli` (applier.py:503-521), whereas the plan drains by appending to `outbound_list` BEFORE `_apply_batch`. These are structurally different drain sites with different failure semantics (mid-batch abort vs. post-batch best-effort). The prior-art claim is misleading and may cause a developer to copy the wrong drain pattern or overlook the different abort/exception semantics.
findings.location: Step 3 (Wiring) — 'Follow the exact pattern applier.py already uses for pending_bug_tickets'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the complete follow-on dict schema (canonical discriminant key name, exhaustive vocabulary of `kind` vs `action` values, which keys are required vs optional) co-located with the applier routing logic — either as a docstring on the routing block, a named TypedDict/dataclass, or a schema comment — and specify whether `suppress_pair` will be migrated to `action` or whether `kind` and `action` are intentionally separate routing axes.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a second follow-on discriminant key (`action`) alongside the existing one (`kind`) in the same applier routing block, but never defines the full follow-on dict schema or the relationship between the two keys. `applier.py:468` checks `follow_on.get('kind')=='suppress_pair'`; the plan adds a new branch checking `follow_on.get('action')=='create_after_hard_delete'`. Both branches read from the same `follow_on` dict with no documented schema specifying whether `kind` and `action` are mutually exclusive, whether both can coexist on the same dict, or which key is authoritative for future follow-on routing. An implementer writing the consumer must guess whether a `create_after_hard_delete` follow_on should also carry `kind=None`, and whether a future follow-on type should use `kind` or `action`.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `follow_on` schema comment/TypedDict in `applier.py` near the routing block (lines 467-474). Define two distinct follow-on shapes: `{"kind": "suppress_pair", ...}` (existing) and `{"action": "create_after_hard_delete", "local_id": ..., "target": ...}` (new). Document that `kind` and `action` are mutually exclusive discriminant keys for different follow-on categories, and that future follow-ons should use `action` (or `kind`). Alternatively, migrate `suppress_pair` to also use `action` as the discriminant key for a single unified routing convention.
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default semantics for 'clear the `.joe-janitor/report` flag' (e.g., 'delete the file entirely' or 'set the `status` field to `resolved`') so the executor doesn't have to guess.
findings.finding: AC #7 instructs 'Clear the `.joe-janitor/report` flag if present' but does not define what 'clear' means — delete the file, truncate it, set a field to a specific value, or something else. This is a defaultable-gap ambiguity (E2 signal #2: implicit acceptance criterion).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) add a live-Jira smoke test (even behind a `RUN_LIVE_JIRA_TESTS=1` env gate) that confirms a real `create_one` call is made and deduplicated, OR (b) explicitly document in the plan why a fake-client E2E is sufficient (e.g., `create_one` itself is separately contract-tested against a live Jira sandbox) and name the covering ticket/test file.
findings.finding: The end-to-end AC (#8) explicitly uses a 'fake acli client' to assert the outbound CREATE. This offline mock bypasses the actual Jira REST boundary that `create_one` exercises (JQL dedup call + POST /rest/api/…). Per E6(f), green offline tests are necessary but not sufficient when the new code path has a live external boundary (Jira) and no rationale is documented for the absence of a live exercise or an explicit deferral to a named child ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC #8: 'OR: document that `create_one`'s JQL-dedup + REST POST are covered by an existing live-Jira contract test in `tests/integration/test_dispatch_one_live.py`; cite that test file as the live coverage rationale.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the `.cache.json` direct-read in `applier.py` as a new file-layout coupling in the plan, and either reference an existing single-ticket accessor or define a small one (e.g., `load_single_ticket(local_id, repo_root)`) so the schema dependency has a single fix point.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a direct file-system read of `.tickets-tracker/<local_id>/.cache.json` inside `applier.py` (the inbound consumer) without acknowledging this as a new coupling to the ticket-store's internal cache schema/layout, nor providing or referencing an accessor abstraction. If the cache schema evolves, `applier.py` silently breaks — this dependency is neither mitigated via an interface nor documented as a coupling risk.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a private helper `_load_local_ticket(local_id: str, repo_root: Path) -> dict | None` in a shared utility module (or in `reconcile_check.py` alongside `load_local_tickets`) that encapsulates the cache-read + fallback logic, and have `applier.py` call it. Reference this helper in the plan scope/files list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (or enforce) that `binding_store`'s save path uses an atomic write (write-to-temp + rename) or holds an exclusive file lock for the full read-modify-write in `bind_confirm`, and document this guarantee in the plan/AC so the TOCTOU window is explicitly closed.
findings.finding: The new `bind_confirm` read-before-overwrite pattern (`old_key = entry.get('jira_key')` BEFORE overwriting, then `reverse.pop(old_key)` in the same save) is a check-then-act sequence. The plan specifies 'SAME save' as the atomicity guarantee but does not state whether the binding store's persistence layer (likely a JSON file) uses atomic rename or file locking. If two reconcile processes ever run concurrently and call `bind_confirm` for the same `local_id`, the `old_key` read and the subsequent pop could race, leaving a dangling or incorrectly-removed reverse entry.
findings.location: Step 5 (Scope: binding_store.py, bind_confirm) / AC: 'bind_confirm deletes the stale reverse[old_key] (read BEFORE overwrite) in the same save'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/reconcile_check.py` to the Scope/files section (read-only, no edit required) or eliminate the fallback by reading `.cache.json` directly (as the primary path already does) and drop the `load_local_tickets` fallback mention.
findings.finding: reconcile_check.py is named in Step 2 as a fallback code path ('falling back to reconcile_check.load_local_tickets only if the cache is absent') but is absent from the Scope/files section, making it an unenumerated import dependency that applier.py would acquire.
findings.location: Step 2 (Reconstruct fields) — 'falling back to reconcile_check.load_local_tickets only if the cache is absent'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm why the direct `.cache.json` read is preferred over calling `reconcile_check.load_local_tickets` (or a thin single-ticket wrapper around it) for the `local_id` lookup in the consumer; if there is no strong reason, switch to the existing helper to avoid duplicating cache-read logic.
findings.finding: The plan specifies a bespoke direct `.cache.json` file-read inside `applier.py` as the PRIMARY lookup mechanism (with `load_local_tickets` only as a fallback), bypassing the already-existing `reconcile_check.load_local_tickets` helper that already reads exactly these per-ticket cache files. This duplicates file-read logic already encapsulated in an existing function without a stated justification for why the hand-rolled path is preferable.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the bespoke `open(<repo_root>/.tickets-tracker/<local_id>/.cache.json)` with a call to a thin helper (e.g. `_load_single_ticket(repo_root, local_id)`) that delegates to `reconcile_check.load_local_tickets` or directly reads via the same path expression already used there, keeping the format logic in one place.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (and explicitly document in Step 3) that the injected outbound CREATE Mutation will be dispatched under the leaf name `outbound_create` through `_apply_batch`, so the `rebar_id_audit` guard does not block the `rebar-id:` label write; if the leaf name is derived differently for injected mutations, add `outbound_create` coverage or extend the authorized-writers set with justification.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the injected outbound CREATE mutation 'flows through the normal batch -> create_one' path without resolving how it will be named at the rebar-id audit-guard boundary. `_audit_rebar_id_label_writes` (rebar_id_audit.py:104-106, enforced inside _apply_batch) only authorizes the leaf named `outbound_create` to write `rebar-id:` labels; `create_one` unconditionally calls `client.add_label(jira_key, f"rebar-id:{local_id}")` on every successful create. If the injected mutation is dispatched under any other leaf name the guard raises `RebarIdLabelWriteError` (fail-closed default), silently killing the re-create. The plan does not mention this gate at all.
findings.suggested_fix: In Step 3, explicitly state that the injected Mutation's `action` must be `MutationAction.create` and `direction` must be `MutationDirection.outbound` so that `typed_dispatch.py:82` maps it to the leaf name `'outbound_create'`, satisfying the `_AUTHORIZED_REBAR_ID_LABEL_WRITERS` guard. Add an AC check: the audit guard is not bypassed during the end-to-end test.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
